skill.name: data-visualization
skill.description: Create charts, graphs, and visual reports from structured data using common Python visualization libraries.
skill.summary: Turn data into clear static or interactive visualizations.
skill.author: 0xkynz

security_audit.summary: Static findings were evaluated against SKILL.md and dismissed as false positives. The cited weak-cryptography lines contain only metadata and prose, and the external-command finding points to a fenced Python matplotlib example rather than shell execution.

low_findings.title: False Positive: Weak Cryptography Pattern
low_findings.description: The cited lines contain the skill description and a plain-language capability sentence. No cryptographic algorithm, hashing operation, credential handling, or security-sensitive code is present.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The exact cited lines are descriptive text about visualization. There is no evidence of MD5, SHA1, DES, RC4, or any other weak cryptographic operation.

low_findings.title: False Positive: External Command Execution
low_findings.description: The cited section is a markdown fenced Python example that imports matplotlib, plots fixed sample data, and saves a chart. It is documentation content, not Ruby or shell backtick execution.
low_findings.confidence_reasoning: The backticks are markdown code-fence delimiters, not executable shell syntax. The Python snippet contains no subprocess calls, shell invocation, network access, or dynamic user input.

content.value_statement: Raw data can be hard to compare, explain, and share. This skill guides Claude, Codex, and Claude Code to create clear charts, reports, and visual summaries.
actual_capabilities: Recommends chart types such as line charts, bar charts, pie charts, scatter plots, histograms, box plots, and heatmaps.
Supports visualization workflows using Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly, and Altair.
Guides creation of multi-axis plots, subplot grids, labels, annotations, and visual themes.
Helps export charts to PNG, SVG, and PDF formats.
Encourages clear titles, labels, accessible color choices, and focused visual design.
limitations: It does not include a data ingestion pipeline or database connector.
It depends on the visualization libraries available in the local environment.
It does not validate statistical assumptions or data quality by itself.
It does not provide hosted dashboards or real-time chart serving.

use_cases.title: Executive Reporting
use_cases.description: Create concise visuals that summarize trends, comparisons, and key metrics for business reviews.

use_cases.title: Research Exploration
use_cases.description: Build exploratory charts that reveal distributions, outliers, relationships, and patterns in datasets.

use_cases.title: Product Metrics Review
use_cases.description: Design charts for usage, retention, conversion, and experiment results with clear labels and annotations.

prompt_templates.title: Choose a Chart
prompt_templates.scenario: Beginner request for chart selection help
prompt_templates.prompt: I have data with these columns: [columns]. Recommend the best chart type and explain what it will show.

prompt_templates.title: Create a Static Chart
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a clean static chart for this dataset using Matplotlib or Seaborn. Include a title, axis labels, and accessible colors.

prompt_templates.title: Compare Multiple Metrics
prompt_templates.scenario: Build a more complex analytical visual
prompt_templates.prompt: Create a visualization that compares [metrics] across [segments] over [time period]. Use subplots or multiple axes only if they improve clarity.

prompt_templates.title: Prepare Publication Output
prompt_templates.prompt: Refine this visualization for a report. Add annotations, improve labels, select an export format, and explain the design choices.

output_examples.input: Show monthly revenue and customer growth in one report visual.
output_examples.output: A combined chart plan with revenue as bars, customer growth as a line, clear monthly labels, and an annotation for the strongest growth month.

output_examples.input: Explore the distribution of support response times.
output_examples.output: A histogram and box plot recommendation that highlights median response time, outliers, and whether the distribution is skewed.

output_examples.input: Make a chart suitable for a PDF board report.
output_examples.output: A static chart design with restrained colors, readable labels, high-resolution export guidance, and a short caption for business context.

best_practices: Start with the decision or question the chart must support.
Use clear labels, direct titles, and accessible color palettes.
Export at a resolution and format that match the final use.
anti_patterns: Do not use complex chart types when a simple comparison is clearer.
Do not combine unrelated metrics without explaining scale and context.
Do not rely on color alone to communicate important differences.

faq.question: What chart libraries does this skill support?
faq.answer: It references Matplotlib, Seaborn, Plotly, and Altair for common static, statistical, interactive, and declarative visualization workflows.

faq.question: Can it create interactive charts?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill lists Plotly and Altair, which can support interactive charts when the environment has those libraries installed.

faq.question: Can it export charts for reports?
faq.answer: Yes. The skill describes export support for PNG, SVG, and PDF formats.

faq.question: Does it clean or transform data?
faq.answer: No. It focuses on visualization guidance. Data cleaning and transformation must be handled separately.

faq.question: Is this skill safe to publish?
faq.answer: Yes. The reviewed static findings were false positives, and no confirmed dangerous behavior was found in SKILL.md.

faq.question: Which users benefit most from this skill?
faq.answer: Analysts, researchers, product managers, and reporting teams can use it to turn datasets into clearer visual explanations.
